refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint or acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The system's functional role is to provide visibility into energy consumption and work output so that stakeholders can assess performance against targets. Achievement of the target is a property of the overall system design and operational execution, not a function the system 'supports' in isolation. This refinement keeps the FR in the problem domain (what must be measured and communicated) and removes the solution-dependent notion of 'supporting achievement,' which presupposes design choices not yet made.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects, calculates, and reports energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved with defined accuracy and frequency over the evaluation period. Confirm that reported values are traceable to raw sensor data and work output logs, and that the reporting interface is accessible to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Safety Manager, Maintenance/Quality).
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
The NGHE solution shall measure or infer total work output (mass moved or equivalent) during operation.
The NGHE solution shall calculate energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work output) from measured inputs.
The NGHE solution shall report energy efficiency metrics to authorized stakeholders at defined intervals.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period
DP2: Deploy real-time energy and tonnage sensors with continuous data aggregation; compute energy_per_ton at configurable intervals; expose metric via reporting interface

DP3: Integrate energy metering into power distribution subsystem; integrate tonnage measurement into load/transport subsystem; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio in a dedicated metering logic module

DP4: Establish energy consumption baseline from historical operational data; measure incremental energy use and tonnage during evaluation period; normalize against baseline to derive efficiency metric
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Integrate energy metering into power distribution subsystem; integrate tonnage measurement into load/transport subsystem; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio in a dedicated metering logic module

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization between energy and tonnage sensors—requires clock alignment or event-driven correlation; mitigate via shared time reference (GPS, NTP, or local sync protocol)

Energy metering placement—if coupled to specific power architecture (AC/DC, voltage level), may limit portability across different equipment; mitigate by defining metering interface abstractly (energy units per time interval)

Tonnage measurement dependency on load cell or scale calibration—if tightly bound to specific equipment geometry, may require recalibration per site; mitigate via modular sensor interface and documented calibration procedure

Ratio computation logic—if embedded in power distribution or transport subsystem, creates functional diffusion; mitigate by isolating computation in dedicated metering logic module with clear input/output contracts
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves moderate decoupling by separating measurement (energy, tonnage) from computation (ratio). Timestamp synchronization is the primary coupling risk; recommend event-driven or periodic sync protocol. Dedicated metering logic module prevents algorithm diffusion and supports independent testing and evolution.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source is measurable at a single point (e.g., fuel tank, electrical meter). Risk: Multi-source energy (e.g., hybrid diesel/electric) requires aggregation logic; recommend explicit energy source inventory in requirements.

Assumption: Tonnage is measured at point of delivery or load-out. Risk: Intermediate transfers, spillage, or measurement error propagate into ratio. Recommend acceptance criteria specify measurement location and tolerance.

Assumption: Evaluation period is fixed and known in advance. Risk: If period is dynamic or overlapping, ratio computation window becomes ambiguous. Recommend explicit definition of evaluation period boundaries and handling of partial-period data.

Assumption: Energy and tonnage data are available at compatible time granularity. Risk: If energy is sampled hourly and tonnage per-minute, aggregation strategy affects accuracy. Recommend data synchronization protocol in DP realization.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan does not prohibit sensor installation or data logging. Risk: If safety policy restricts equipment modification, sensor placement may be constrained. Recommend early coordination with safety stakeholder.

Assumption: E_target is achievable with current equipment and operational practices. Risk: If target is unrealistic, measurement system will report non-compliance without actionable improvement path. Recommend baseline study before evaluation period.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Energy source(s) to be metered (fuel, electricity, or both); (2) Tonnage measurement location and method (load cell, scale, or derived from operational data); (3) Evaluation period definition (start/end date, handling of partial periods); (4) Data synchronization tolerance (e.g., ±5 min timestamp skew acceptable); (5) Baseline or reference energy_per_ton for comparison. This will reduce ambiguity in DP realization and support clearer acceptance criteria. Additionally, confirm that worksite safety plan permits sensor installation and that E_target has been validated as achievable through a pilot or baseline study.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized metering logic module that integrates energy metering from power distribution subsystem, tonnage measurement from load/transport subsystem, synchronizes timestamps, and computes efficiency ratios

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports computed energy efficiency ratios, synchronized metering data, and aggregated metrics to external monitoring systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives metering configuration requests and measurement sampling directives from task orchestration

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to retrieve real-time energy and tonnage sensor data; may also receive remote metering commands

module_containment_scores.notes: Strong containment: NGHE Solution is the logical home for cross-subsystem metering orchestration and ratio computation. Energy metering and tonnage measurement originate in Vehicle subsystem but synchronization and ratio logic belong at solution level. Minor diffusion: must interface with Vehicle and Teleoperation to fetch sensor data; Telemetry export is expected external coupling.

Weak containment: Vehicle owns power distribution and load/transport subsystems but does not own the metering logic orchestration layer. Placing ratio computation here would require Vehicle to own cross-subsystem synchronization logic, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion: metering results must diffuse to Teleoperation and external systems; timestamp synchronization logic would be misplaced in a single vehicle context.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a control/monitoring interface, not a data aggregation or metering logic layer. Placing metering computation here conflates operator interface concerns with backend analytics. High diffusion: metering data must flow to Vehicle subsystems and external Telemetry; logic would be scattered across control and data paths.

Poor containment: Safety Services is orthogonal to metering logic. While energy efficiency may inform safety thresholds, the metering computation itself is not a safety function. High diffusion: metering data would need to diffuse to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Telemetry; safety-specific logic would be entangled with general metering.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate boundary because it sits at the system integration layer, above individual subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). It can own the metering logic module as a dedicated internal component that orchestrates data collection from power distribution and load/transport subsystems, performs timestamp synchronization, and computes efficiency ratios. This placement respects the functional hierarchy: Vehicle subsystems provide raw sensor data; NGHE Solution provides cross-subsystem analytics and metering orchestration.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle subsystem is tightly coupled and does not expose clean energy and tonnage measurement interfaces, the metering logic module may struggle to access sensor data without deep coupling into Vehicle internals.
Risk: Timestamp synchronization across distributed subsystems (power distribution, load/transport) may require additional clock synchronization infrastructure not yet defined in the DP.

Risk: If Teleoperation requires real-time metering feedback for operator display, latency in the NGHE Solution layer could introduce unacceptable delays; this may necessitate a secondary metering path or caching strategy.

Risk: The DP assumes a 'dedicated metering logic module' but does not specify whether this is a single monolithic component or a distributed set of microservices; unclear packaging could lead to diffusion across multiple IM boundaries.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine DP3 to explicitly state: 'Establish a dedicated Metering Logic Module within NGHE Solution that (a) subscribes to energy metering events from Vehicle power distribution subsystem via a standardized Telemetry interface, (b) subscribes to tonnage measurement events from Vehicle load/transport subsystem via the same Telemetry interface, (c) maintains a synchronized clock or uses network-provided timestamps, (d) computes efficiency ratios (energy per unit tonnage) on a configurable schedule, and (e) publishes results via Telemetry to external systems and SafetyAlert for threshold violations.' This revision clarifies module ownership, interface contracts, and reduces ambiguity about data flow and synchronization responsibility.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 correctly addresses the FR by specifying three concrete mechanisms: (1) energy metering integration into power distribution, (2) tonnage measurement integration into load/transport, and (3) synchronized timestamp computation of the ratio. The DP is solution-oriented and traces directly to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps detected between functional requirement and design parameter.

diffusion_risks: DP3 specifies 'dedicated metering logic module' but does not clarify whether this is a single monolithic component or distributed microservices. Ambiguous packaging risks diffusion across multiple IM boundaries (e.g., logic split between NGHE Solution core and Vehicle subsystem adapters).

Timestamp synchronization responsibility is underspecified in DP3. If synchronization logic must span power distribution and load/transport subsystems within Vehicle, the DP may inadvertently diffuse synchronization concerns across Vehicle internal boundaries without explicit ownership.

DP3 states 'integrate energy metering into power distribution subsystem' and 'integrate tonnage measurement into load/transport subsystem' but does not clarify whether these integrations are DP-level design decisions (owned by Vehicle) or IM-level packaging decisions (owned by NGHE Solution). This ambiguity creates a DP<->IM bridge risk.

Real-time metering feedback path to Teleoperation is not addressed in DP3. If Teleoperation requires low-latency metering display, the DP may require a secondary metering path or caching strategy not yet captured, risking diffusion of ratio computation logic across NGHE Solution and Teleoperation boundaries.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary (containment_score 0.85) for the metering logic module because it sits at the system integration layer and can orchestrate cross-subsystem data collection without violating separation of concerns. However, the DP must be refined to explicitly state: (a) which subsystem owns the energy metering interface (power distribution), (b) which subsystem owns the tonnage measurement interface (load/transport), (c) whether NGHE Solution owns the timestamp synchronization logic or delegates it to Vehicle, and (d) whether a secondary real-time metering path is required for Teleoperation. Current DP lacks these clarifications, creating ambiguity about IM boundary placement and interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is feasible in principle, but the current IM specification does not clearly define: (1) the data source and quality assurance for energy metering (which power distribution subsystem component?), (2) the data source and quality assurance for tonnage measurement (which load/transport subsystem component?), (3) the timestamp synchronization mechanism and its accuracy tolerance, and (4) the computation schedule and aggregation window for ratio calculation. Without these details, verification traceability from FR to IM is incomplete.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation clarity is moderate. NGHE Solution is identified as the ownership boundary, but the allocation of sub-responsibilities is unclear: Does NGHE Solution own the metering logic module entirely, or does it share ownership with Vehicle subsystems for sensor interface adaptation? Does NGHE Solution own timestamp synchronization, or is this delegated to Vehicle? Does NGHE Solution own the Telemetry export interface, or is this a shared responsibility with an external Telemetry subsystem? The recommended revision addresses these gaps by explicitly stating subscription contracts, synchronization responsibility, and publication targets.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP3 to explicitly state: 'Establish a dedicated Metering Logic Module within NGHE Solution that (a) subscribes to energy metering events from Vehicle power distribution subsystem via a standardized Energy Telemetry interface, (b) subscribes to tonnage measurement events from Vehicle load/transport subsystem via a standardized Load Telemetry interface, (c) maintains synchronized timestamps using network-provided NTP or Vehicle-provided clock signals with documented accuracy tolerance (e.g., ±100ms), (d) computes efficiency ratios (energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved) on a configurable schedule (e.g., per-shift, per-task, real-time), and (e) publishes results via Telemetry to external monitoring systems and SafetyAlert for threshold violations.' This revision clarifies module ownership, interface contracts, synchronization responsibility, and reduces ambiguity about data flow.

Add a secondary DP clause addressing real-time metering feedback: 'If Teleoperation requires real-time metering display with latency <500ms, establish a secondary metering cache within Teleoperation that subscribes to efficiency ratio updates from NGHE Solution Metering Logic Module and maintains a rolling window of recent values. This avoids diffusion of ratio computation logic into Teleoperation.' This prevents diffusion risk by explicitly separating computation (NGHE Solution) from display (Teleoperation).

Specify timestamp synchronization infrastructure in DP3: 'Timestamp synchronization shall use one of: (1) Vehicle-provided synchronized clock accessible to both power distribution and load/transport subsystems, (2) network-provided NTP with documented accuracy, or (3) event-based correlation using Vehicle CAN bus or similar time-stamped message bus. Document the chosen mechanism and its accuracy tolerance in the design specification.' This removes ambiguity about synchronization ownership and feasibility.

Clarify the metering logic module's internal structure: 'The Metering Logic Module shall be implemented as a single cohesive component (not distributed across multiple IM boundaries) that owns: (a) event subscription logic, (b) timestamp synchronization logic, (c) ratio computation logic, and (d) result publication logic. All four responsibilities shall reside within the NGHE Solution boundary to prevent diffusion.' This prevents the risk of scattering the DP across multiple IM boundaries.

Add acceptance criteria traceability: 'The Metering Logic Module shall compute energy_per_ton with measurement accuracy ±5% (or per worksite safety plan requirements) over the agreed evaluation period. Verification shall include: (1) energy metering calibration certificate from power distribution subsystem, (2) tonnage measurement calibration certificate from load/transport subsystem, (3) timestamp synchronization accuracy test results, and (4) ratio computation unit test coverage ≥95%.' This ensures FR-to-IM verification feasibility.

decision.justification: The FR->DP gate passes, confirming logical correctness of the design parameter. However, the DP->IM gate fails with five critical diffusion risks and the FR->IM gate fails with incomplete verification traceability. The root cause is that DP3 lacks explicit architectural clarity on module ownership, interface contracts, synchronization responsibility, and data flow paths. Revising DP3 to incorporate the five recommended clarifications will eliminate ambiguity about IM boundary placement and enable proper sub-FR decomposition at the next level. Proceeding to sub-FR derivation without this revision risks propagating architectural ambiguity into lower levels, creating diffusion and verification gaps that are expensive to remediate late in design. Given high safety criticality, architectural clarity is non-negotiable before decomposition.

revise.why: DP3 must be refined to explicitly address: (1) Metering Logic Module ownership and internal cohesion (single component, not distributed), (2) subscription contracts to Vehicle power distribution and load/transport subsystems with standardized interface names, (3) timestamp synchronization mechanism and accuracy tolerance (±100ms or per safety plan), (4) computation schedule and aggregation window (per-shift, per-task, or real-time), (5) publication targets and latency requirements for Teleoperation real-time display (if required), and (6) secondary metering cache design if Teleoperation latency <500ms is mandated. These revisions transform DP3 from a logical solution sketch into an architecturally grounded design parameter that clearly maps to IM boundaries and enables verification traceability. The five recommended revisions in the gate output provide concrete language for this refinement.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary for Metering Logic Module (containment_score 0.85 confirmed by DP->IM gate).
Vehicle power distribution and load/transport subsystems expose standardized telemetry interfaces (Energy Telemetry, Load Telemetry) that NGHE Solution can subscribe to without modification.
Timestamp synchronization is feasible via Vehicle-provided clock, NTP, or CAN bus time-stamping with accuracy tolerance ≤±100ms.
Teleoperation may require real-time metering display; if so, a secondary caching strategy is acceptable to avoid diffusion of ratio computation logic.
Energy metering and tonnage measurement subsystems are independently calibrated and provide data with ±5% accuracy or better.
Safety criticality of energy efficiency monitoring is high; verification traceability and measurement accuracy are non-negotiable.
open_risks: Timestamp synchronization mechanism not yet selected (Vehicle clock vs. NTP vs. CAN bus); selection impacts IM design and interface contracts.
Real-time metering feedback latency requirement for Teleoperation not yet confirmed; if <500ms is mandated, secondary caching DP clause is required.

Energy metering and tonnage measurement subsystem interfaces not yet formally specified; DP revision must reference standardized interface contracts (e.g., Energy Telemetry v1.0, Load Telemetry v1.0).
Computation schedule (per-shift, per-task, real-time) not yet finalized; impacts data aggregation window and storage requirements in Metering Logic Module.
Verification plan for acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) not yet detailed; DP revision must include calibration certificate requirements and unit test coverage targets.
Potential diffusion of synchronization logic across Vehicle internal boundaries if NGHE Solution does not own explicit synchronization responsibility; DP revision must clarify ownership.
evidence_needed: Refined DP3 incorporating all five recommended revisions from gate output, with explicit module ownership, interface contracts, synchronization mechanism, computation schedule, and Teleoperation latency handling.
Formal specification of Energy Telemetry and Load Telemetry interfaces (message schema, update frequency, accuracy guarantees) from Vehicle subsystems.
Timestamp synchronization mechanism selection document (Vehicle clock, NTP, or CAN bus) with accuracy tolerance justification.
Teleoperation real-time metering display latency requirement (if any) from Teleoperation subsystem stakeholders.
Verification plan traceability matrix linking FR acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) to IM-level test cases, calibration procedures, and measurement accuracy targets.

Safety criticality assessment confirming that metering logic module failures (e.g., timestamp drift, ratio computation errors) do not violate safety constraints; if safety-critical, FMEA or similar hazard analysis required.
